**[STATE OF NEW JERSEY]{.underline}**

**[DEPARTMENT OF LABOR AND WORKFORCE DEVELOPMENT]{.underline}**

**[Division of Wage and Hour Compliance]{.underline}**

**COMPLAINT SUMMARY AND NOTICE TO EMPLOYER**

**Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Complainant:**                      **Carla Muñoz, Warehouse Associate, Edison, NJ**
  ------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------
  **Respondent Employer:**              Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.

  **Employer EIN:**                     82-3461097

  **Employer Address:**                 400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300, Edison, NJ 08817

  **Statute at Issue:**                 New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.*

  **Complaint Received by Employer:**   February 3, 2025
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[Section 1 --- Nature of Complaint]{.underline}**

The above-named Complainant, Carla Muñoz, has filed a complaint with the New Jersey Department of Labor and Workforce Development, Division of Wage and Hour Compliance, pursuant to the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.* (the \"Act\").

Ms. Muñoz is employed by Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. (\"Respondent\" or \"Employer\") as a Warehouse Associate at the Employer\'s Edison, New Jersey facility located at 400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300, Edison, NJ 08817. Complainant alleges that on or about a date in late January 2025, she submitted a request to use accrued earned sick leave for the purpose of caring for her domestic partner\'s child, who was experiencing an illness. At the time of the request, Complainant states she had a sufficient balance of accrued earned sick leave hours available for use.

Complainant alleges that her request was denied by her direct supervisor, who informed her that the Employer\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy permits the use of sick leave only for the care of a \"spouse, child, or parent\" and that a domestic partner\'s child did not qualify as a covered family member under the terms of that policy.

The Act defines \"family member\" broadly under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 to include, among other relationships, a child of a domestic partner, a domestic partner or civil union partner of the employee, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, any blood relative, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship. The Employer\'s policy language limiting covered family members to \"spouse, child, or parent\" is narrower than the statutory definition and, as applied in this instance, excluded a relationship --- the child of a domestic partner --- that is expressly covered by the Act.

As a result of the denial, Complainant states she was required to take unpaid time off or use accrued paid time off (PTO) from a separate leave bank in order to care for the child. Complainant asserts that the denial constituted an improper restriction on her right to use earned sick leave for a covered purpose under the Act, namely the care of a family member during illness, which is a use expressly permitted by N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3.

**[Section 2 --- Statutory Basis and Alleged Violations]{.underline}**

This complaint is brought under the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.*, which became effective October 29, 2018. The Act requires every employer in the State of New Jersey to provide earned sick leave to employees and to permit the use of such leave for specified purposes.

**Alleged Violation 1 --- Improperly Restrictive Definition of \"Family Member\"**

The Employer\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy defines covered family members as \"spouse, child, or parent.\" This definition excludes domestic partners, children of domestic partners, civil union partners, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, other blood relatives, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship --- all of whom are included within the definition of \"family member\" under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1. By maintaining and applying this narrower definition, the Employer denied Ms. Muñoz the use of accrued earned sick leave for the care of her domestic partner\'s child, a covered family member under the Act.

**Alleged Violation 2 --- Denial of Earned Sick Leave for a Covered Purpose**

The Employer denied the use of accrued earned sick leave for a purpose expressly permitted by the Act, namely the care of a family member during illness. Pursuant to N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3, an employer is required to permit the use of earned sick leave for the following purposes:

> \(a\) Time needed for the diagnosis, care, treatment of, or recovery from, the employee\'s own mental or physical illness, injury, or other adverse health condition, or for preventive medical care for the employee;
>
> \(b\) Time needed for the employee to aid or care for a family member of the employee during diagnosis, care, treatment of, or recovery from, the family member\'s mental or physical illness, injury, or other adverse health condition, or for preventive medical care for the family member;
>
> \(c\) Absence necessary due to circumstances resulting from the employee, or a family member of the employee, being a victim of domestic or sexual violence, including time for obtaining related medical attention, services from a designated domestic violence agency or other victim services organization, psychological or other counseling, relocation, or participation in related legal proceedings;
>
> \(d\) Time during which the employee is not able to work because of a closure of the employee\'s workplace, or the school or place of care of a child of the employee, by order of a public official due to an epidemic or other public health emergency, or because of the issuance by a public health authority of a determination that the presence in the community of the employee, or a member of the employee\'s family in need of care by the employee, would jeopardize the health of others; and
>
> \(e\) Time needed by the employee in connection with a child of the employee to attend a school-related conference, meeting, function, or other event requested or required by a school administrator, teacher, or other professional staff member responsible for the child\'s education, or to attend a meeting regarding care provided to the child in connection with the child\'s health conditions or disability.

The Employer\'s policy, as described in the complaint and as reflected in the Employer\'s Employee Handbook, states that paid sick leave may be used for \"the employee\'s own illness, injury, or medical appointment.\" This language omits several categories of covered uses required by the Act, including care for family members during illness or for preventive medical care, absences related to domestic or sexual violence, absences due to public health emergency closures, and attendance at school-related functions for an employee\'s child. The denial of Ms. Muñoz\'s request is a direct consequence of these omissions.

The Employer is hereby requested to provide a written response to this complaint within thirty (30) days of receipt. The Division notes that violations of the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act may subject an employer to penalties pursuant to N.J.S.A. 34:11D-7, including fines, administrative penalties, and orders of restitution.

**[Section 3 --- Relief Sought]{.underline}**

Based on the foregoing, Complainant Carla Muñoz seeks the following relief:

> \(a\) Payment for the unpaid time off taken, or restoration of the PTO hours used, in connection with the care of her domestic partner\'s child during the period at issue in late January 2025;
>
> \(b\) Amendment of the Employer\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy to bring it into compliance with the Act\'s definition of \"family member\" under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 and to include all covered uses of earned sick leave as set forth in N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3; and
>
> \(c\) Any applicable statutory penalties authorized under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-7.

The Department of Labor and Workforce Development reserves the right to conduct a broader investigation of the Employer\'s sick leave practices, policies, and records to determine whether additional violations of the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act have occurred.

**HARTWELL & CABOT LLP** *Attorneys at Law*

One Gateway Center, Suite 1200 Newark, New Jersey 07102 Tel: (973) 555-4800 \| Fax: (973) 555-4801 www.hartwellcabot.com

February 21, 2025

*Via First-Class Mail and Electronic Submission*

New Jersey Department of Labor and Workforce Development Division of Wage and Hour Compliance PO Box 389 Trenton, NJ 08625-0389

> **Re: Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117 --- Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.**

Dear Sir or Madam:

**[1. Introduction and Representation]{.underline}**

This firm represents Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. (\"Vantage\" or the \"Company\") in connection with the above-referenced complaint filed by Carla Muñoz pursuant to the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.* (the \"Act\"). The Company received the complaint on February 3, 2025, and this letter constitutes its timely response within the thirty-day period provided.

Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. is a Delaware corporation with its principal offices located at 400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300, Edison, New Jersey 08817. Vantage operates a freight brokerage, warehousing, and last-mile delivery business with facilities in Edison and at 78 Doremus Avenue, Newark, NJ 07105. The Company employs approximately 890 individuals in New Jersey, of whom approximately 520 are hourly employees and approximately 370 are salaried. Ms. Muñoz is employed as a Warehouse Associate at the Edison facility and has been in that role for the duration of her employment with Vantage. She is classified as a full-time hourly employee and is eligible for earned sick leave benefits under the Company\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy.

**[2. Factual Background and Company Position]{.underline}**

The Company\'s current Paid Sick Leave Policy was adopted and last updated on January 15, 2022, as part of a comprehensive revision to the Company\'s Employee Handbook. The policy was reviewed at that time by the Company\'s internal Human Resources department, under the direction of Marcus Delgado, Director of Human Resources.

Under the policy, all full-time employees accrue one hour of paid sick leave for every 40 hours worked, up to a maximum of 40 hours per benefit year. The policy states that paid sick leave may be used for \"the employee\'s own illness, injury, or medical appointment.\" With respect to leave taken on behalf of others, the policy permits use of paid sick leave for the care of a covered family member and defines such family members as a \"spouse, child, or parent\" of the employee.

The Company acknowledges that the complaint raises questions regarding whether its policy language fully reflects the scope of the Act\'s requirements. At the time of drafting in January 2022, the Company believed that the family member definition and the described covered uses were consistent with its obligations under New Jersey law. However, in light of the complaint, the Company is reviewing its policy language to ensure alignment with the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, including the statutory definition of \"family member\" set forth in N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1.

The Company further acknowledges that the policy\'s enumeration of covered uses --- limited to \"the employee\'s own illness, injury, or medical appointment\" --- may not fully reflect the range of permissible uses under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3. The Company is evaluating whether additional covered uses, including the care of family members during illness, absences related to domestic or sexual violence, absences necessitated by public health emergency closures, and attendance at school-related functions, should be expressly enumerated in the policy to ensure that employees and supervisors have clear and complete guidance regarding the permitted uses of earned sick leave.

Regarding the specific incident at issue, the denial of Ms. Muñoz\'s request was made by a front-line supervisor who relied upon the express terms of the written policy. This decision was not made with any intent to deprive Ms. Muñoz of a statutory right, nor was it the product of any animus toward Ms. Muñoz or her domestic partner. The Company\'s HR department, under Mr. Delgado\'s direction, has consistently applied the Paid Sick Leave Policy as written and has administered sick leave requests in accordance with the policy\'s stated terms.

The Company does not concede that a violation of the Act has occurred. However, the Company acknowledges that the complaint raises legitimate questions warranting a thorough review of its policy language and administrative practices, and it is committed to addressing those questions in a diligent and timely manner.

**[3. Remedial Steps Under Consideration]{.underline}**

The Company has engaged Hartwell & Cabot LLP to conduct a comprehensive review of its Paid Sick Leave Policy for compliance with the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act and applicable earned sick leave requirements in all jurisdictions in which Vantage operates. Specifically, the Company is undertaking the following steps:

> \(a\) Reviewing the definition of \"family member\" in the Paid Sick Leave Policy to determine whether it should be expanded to align with the statutory definition under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1, which the Company understands to include the employee\'s child, parent, spouse, domestic partner, civil union partner, grandparent, grandchild, sibling, child or parent of a domestic partner or civil union partner, any other individual related to the employee by blood, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship;
>
> \(b\) Reviewing the list of permissible uses for earned sick leave to ensure that all statutorily required categories of use under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3 are expressly included in the policy;
>
> \(c\) Evaluating whether Ms. Muñoz should be made whole for any lost wages or PTO hours expended as a result of the denial of her earned sick leave request in late January 2025; and
>
> \(d\) Planning and scheduling training for all supervisors and HR staff at the Edison and Newark facilities on the proper administration of earned sick leave under New Jersey law, including the correct identification of covered family members and permissible leave purposes.

The Company anticipates completing its policy review and implementing any necessary revisions by July 1, 2025, which is the target date for issuance of a revised Employee Handbook covering all Company locations.

**[4. Request for Cooperation and Conclusion]{.underline}**

The Company respectfully requests the opportunity to resolve this matter cooperatively and to demonstrate its good faith efforts to achieve full compliance with the Act. In particular, the Company asks that the Department allow a reasonable period --- through the July 1, 2025 handbook revision target --- for the Company to complete its review, update its policies, conduct supervisor training, and address Ms. Muñoz\'s individual claim before any enforcement action is initiated.

Vantage is willing to provide any additional information, documentation, or records that the Department may request in connection with its review of this matter, including payroll records, leave accrual records, and copies of all relevant policy documents.

For all matters related to this complaint, please direct correspondence to the undersigned at the contact information provided below. Catherine Ng, Partner at Hartwell & Cabot LLP, serves as supervising attorney on this matter and may also be reached at this office. For any corporate-level inquiries, Rebecca Thorsen, SVP and General Counsel of Vantage Logistics Group, Inc., may be contacted directly at the Company\'s Edison headquarters.

We appreciate the Department\'s consideration and look forward to working toward an appropriate resolution.

Respectfully submitted,

HARTWELL & CABOT LLP

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Daniel Ikeda Senior Associate One Gateway Center, Suite 1200 Newark, New Jersey 07102 Tel: (973) 555-4822

Email: dikeda@hartwellcabot.com

 state programs, or whether it was always intended to sit alongside them.

I think we need Catherine Ng's team at Hartwell & Cabot to look at this as pa=
rt of the broader compliance review we're about to kick off. This may be a bi=
gger structural issue than just CT =E2=80=94 NJ has TDI, NY has DBL, and all =
four of the expansion states have their own paid medical leave programs. If o=
ur self-funded plan doesn't meet the state minimums and we haven't properly o=
pted out, we could have exposure across the board.

**Action item for you:** Can you compile a list of all employee inquiries abo=
ut paid medical leave for their own conditions =E2=80=94 CT, NJ, and NY =E2=
=80=94 going back 12 months? I need dates, employee names, and how HR respond=
ed in each case. This will go to Hartwell & Cabot as part of the engagement.

**CO PTO Payout**

Your CO candidate is likely correct. I recall from law school that Colorado h=
as strong wage protection laws requiring payout of accrued vacation, and I be=
lieve that's still the case =E2=80=94 though I haven't researched it recently=
. Our handbook only addresses CT because that was the only state where we had=
 this issue when the policy was last updated in January 2022. We definitely n=
eed to update the policy for CO, and we should check MA, OR, and WA before th=
ose facilities open.

That said, I do NOT want to hold the CO offer letters. They should go out on =
schedule. But make sure you remove the blanket "no payout" language and inclu=
de a note that the employee handbook is being revised and that the company wi=
ll comply with all applicable state laws regarding PTO/vacation payout. Refer=
ence Colorado law specifically. Don't send letters that flatly state we don't=
 pay out PTO =E2=80=94 that's almost certainly wrong for CO.

This is another item for the Hartwell & Cabot engagement. I'm planning to for=
mally open the engagement with Catherine Ng the week of April 14, 2025, and t=
his will be on the list.

**Notice Requirement**

You were absolutely right to approve the leave. The 30-day notice requirement=
 in the handbook doesn't address unforeseeable leave situations at all, which=
 is a drafting gap we need to fix. FMLA requires 30 days for foreseeable leav=
e but only "as soon as practicable" for unforeseeable leave. I'd expect the s=
tate programs have similar carve-outs. Our policy's silence on unforeseeable =
leave could be read as requiring 30 days in all cases, which would be unreaso=
nable and likely non-compliant.

**Interim guidance:** Please instruct HR staff at all locations that they sho=
uld not deny or delay leave when the need is unforeseeable and the employee p=
rovides notice as soon as practicable =E2=80=94 regardless of what the writte=
n policy currently says. I know that's an awkward position to put people in, =
but we can't have employees being turned away for legitimate emergencies.

The policy language needs to be rewritten to address foreseeable vs. unforese=
eable notice requirements for each state, and this will be part of the handbo=
ok revision targeting July 1, 2025.

**Next Steps**

I want to get the Hartwell & Cabot engagement formalized as soon as possible.=
 All three of your issues are in scope. We have an overall compliance remedia=
tion budget of $175,000, and we'll need to prioritize, but all of these feel =
like top-tier items to me.

Please send me: (1) the list of CT/NJ/NY medical leave inquiries as described=
 above, (2) the draft CO offer letters for my review before they go out, and =
(3) a brief memo on the notice requirement incidents =E2=80=94 who was involv=
ed, how it was handled, and whether any employees expressed dissatisfaction w=
ith the response.

One thing I'm keeping an eye on: the target for the revised Employee Handbook=
 is July 1, 2025, but the MA facility opens August 1, 2025. That's not a lot =
of runway. We need to make sure the handbook is done, reviewed, and distribut=
ed before the first MA employee starts.

We'll get ahead of this.

Rebecca

Rebecca Thorsen
Senior Vice President & General Counsel
Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.
400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300
Edison, NJ 08817
(732) 555-4210 | rthorsen@vantagelogistics.com

From: Marcus Delgado <mdelgado@vantagelogistics.com>
To: Rebecca Thorsen <rthorsen@vantagelogistics.com>
Date: March 14, 2025, 10:32 AM
Subject: RE: RE: Leave Policy Concerns =E2=80=94 CT Employee Inquiries & CO E=
arly Hires

Rebecca,

Thanks for the quick guidance on all three items =E2=80=94 very helpful. I'll=
 get moving on the action items.

**CT/NJ/NY Inquiry List**

I did a quick scan and have already identified at least seven employee inquir=
ies about paid medical leave for their own conditions across CT and NJ in the=
 last 12 months =E2=80=94 four in Bridgeport (CT), two here in Edison (NJ), a=
nd one in Queens (NY). I'll compile the full list with dates, names, and HR r=
esponses by end of next week.

One thing that came up as I was pulling this together: in the NJ cases, emplo=
yees were also pointed to the voluntary STD through Keystone Benefit Administ=
rators, even though NJ has its own TDI program. I'm not sure our HR staff in =
NJ even know that NJ TDI exists as a separate benefit from our self-funded pl=
an. They've been telling employees to file through Keystone. If the payroll w=
ithholdings through PinnacleHR are going to the state TDI fund, shouldn't emp=
loyees be filing claims through the state?

This might be a bigger misdirection issue than I initially thought =E2=80=94 =
it's not just CT. If HR staff at multiple sites are sending employees to a vo=
luntary, employee-paid STD plan when those employees are already paying into =
state programs that cover their own medical conditions, that's a real problem.

**CO Offer Letters**

Will do =E2=80=94 I'll revise the three offer letters to remove the blanket "=
no PTO payout" language and add a compliance savings clause referencing Color=
ado law. Do you want to review them before they go out, or should I just send=
 them to you after for your files?

One other thing I thought of: should we also check whether our 5-day carryove=
r cap on PTO is an issue in CO? If Colorado requires payout of all accrued va=
cation, limiting carryover to 5 days might also get scrutinized =E2=80=94 it =
could look like we're using the cap to minimize the payout obligation. Not su=
re if that's a real legal concern, but it seemed worth raising.

**Notice Requirement**

I've instructed the Bridgeport HR team to follow the interim guidance on unfo=
reseeable leave. I also checked with the NJ and NY HR leads =E2=80=94 they co=
nfirmed they've generally been flexible in practice when employees have emerg=
encies, but they acknowledged the written policy doesn't give them clear auth=
ority to waive the 30-day requirement. They've basically been making judgment=
 calls without a framework.

That inconsistency concerns me. If one site is strict and another is flexible=
, we could have discrimination claims on top of the compliance issue. The soo=
ner we get the policy language updated, the better.

I'll have all requested materials ready by March 21. One question: should we =
loop in Priya Samuelson at PinnacleHR Solutions on the withholding/state prog=
ram questions? They handle the actual contribution remittances, so they'd be =
able to tell us exactly where the money is going and whether employees are se=
t up to file state claims. Might save some time.

Best,

Marcus Delgado
Director of Human Resources
Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.
400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300
Edison, NJ 08817
(732) 555-4180 | mdelgado@vantagelogistics.com

From: Rebecca Thorsen <rthorsen@vantagelogistics.com>
To: Marcus Delgado <mdelgado@vantagelogistics.com>
Date: March 17, 2025, 4:48 PM
Subject: RE: RE: RE: Leave Policy Concerns =E2=80=94 CT Employee Inquiries & =
CO Early Hires

Marcus,

March 21 works for the materials =E2=80=94 thank you.

**CT/NJ Misdirection Issue**

This is exactly the kind of thing that worries me. This absolutely needs to g=
o to Hartwell & Cabot. We may need to look at whether employees who were misd=
irected to Keystone instead of the state TDI/DBL programs have grounds for co=
mplaints or retroactive claims. The Mu=C3=B1oz complaint is a reminder that o=
ur employees are willing to go to state agencies when they feel they haven't =
gotten what they're entitled to.

If we're withholding contributions for NJ TDI, NY DBL, and CT PL but our HR s=
taff aren't directing employees to file claims through those state programs, =
we have a real problem. The self-funded plan was supposed to supplement, not =
replace, the state programs =E2=80=94 at least that's how I understood it whe=
n we set it up. But I'm no longer confident that's how it was implemented. I =
need Catherine Ng's team to untangle what we actually have versus what we wer=
e supposed to have, and where the gaps are.

**CO Offer Letters**

Please send them to me for review before they go out. I do not want any lette=
rs going to CO employees without legal review given the current state of flux=
. I'll turn them around quickly.

Your carryover cap question is a good one =E2=80=94 I'll add it to the list f=
or Hartwell & Cabot.

I also want to check whether Massachusetts requires PTO payout upon separatio=
n, since that facility opens just one month after the revised handbook target=
 date (MA opening August 1, 2025; handbook target July 1, 2025). If MA also r=
equires payout, we need to have the policy fixed before those first MA employ=
ees start. We can't afford another situation where the handbook says one thin=
g and the law says another.

**PinnacleHR**

Hold off on looping in Priya Samuelson for now. I want to wait until after we=
've engaged Hartwell & Cabot formally =E2=80=94 I'd rather have outside couns=
el's guidance on what questions to ask the payroll vendor, rather than alerti=
ng the vendor to potential issues prematurely. We need to understand our own =
legal position first before we start having those conversations.

**Timeline**

I'll be setting up the Hartwell & Cabot engagement in the first two weeks of =
April. Deliverables deadline from outside counsel will likely be mid-May =E2=
=80=94 targeting May 19, 2025 =E2=80=94 so we should have answers on all of t=
hese issues well before the July 1 handbook revision date.

In the meantime, the interim guidance stands across all sites: (1) do not den=
y or delay leave for unforeseeable situations based on the 30-day notice requ=
irement, and (2) do not turn away employees who ask about paid medical leave =
for their own conditions without at least checking whether a state program ap=
plies. If HR staff aren't sure, they should escalate to you, and you can brin=
g it to me.

Thanks for staying on top of this, Marcus. More to come.

Rebecca

Rebecca Thorsen
Senior Vice President & General Counsel
Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.
400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300
Edison, NJ 08817

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")

result:
"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

Checks ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness for every part, [Content_Types].xml
presence, and relationship consistency (no dangling rIds). Does NOT do XSD
schema validation in v1 — defer until ECMA-376 XSDs are vendored fresh from
ECMA-International.

Usage: python validate.py file.docx
Exit 0 = valid; non-zero = errors printed to stderr.
"""
import sys
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
from lxml import etree


def validate(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not path.exists():
        return [f"File not found: {path}"]
    if not zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
        return [f"Not a valid ZIP: {path}"]

    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        names = set(z.namelist())

        if "[Content_Types].xml" not in names:
            errors.append("Missing [Content_Types].xml")
            return errors

        # XML well-formedness for every .x

pandoc 3.1.11.1
---

---docx validate---
"""Validate a .docx for delivery.

Checks ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness for every part, [Content_Types].xml
presence, and relationship consistency (no dangling rIds). Does NOT do XSD
schema validation in v1 — defer until ECMA-376 XSDs are vendored fresh from
ECMA-International.

Usage: python validate.py file.docx
Exit 0 = valid; non-zero = errors printed to stderr.
"""
import sys
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
from lxml import etree


def validate(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not path.exists():
        return [f"File not found: {path}"]
    if not zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
        return [f"Not a valid ZIP: {path}"]

    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        names = set(z.namelist())

        if "[Content_Types].xml" not in names:
            errors.append("Missing [Content_Types].xml")
            return errors

        # XML well-formedness for every .xml and .rels part
        for name in names:
            if name.endswith(".xml") or name.endswith(".rels"):
                try:
                    etree.fromstring(z.read(name))
                except etree.XMLSyntaxError as e:
                    errors.append(f"Malformed XML in {name}: {e}")

        # Relationship target consistency

"""Build the Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Matrix for Vantage Logistics Group.

Prepared by Hartwell & Cabot LLP. Privileged & Confidential / Attorney Work Product.
"""
import openpyxl
from openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment, Border, Side
from openpyxl.utils import get_column_letter

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Style constants
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
RED_FILL    = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="FFC7CE")
RED_FONT    = Font(color="9C0006", size=9, name="Calibri")
YELLOW_FILL = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="FFEB9C")
YELLOW_FONT = Font(color="9C6500", size=9, name="Calibri")
GREEN_FILL  = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="C6EFCE")
GREEN_FONT  = Font(color="006100", size=9, name="Calibri")

HEADER_FILL = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="1F3864")   # dark navy
HEADER_FONT = Font(color="FFFFFF", bold=True, size=10, name="Calibri")
SUBHDR_FILL = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="2E5496")
SUBHDR_FONT = Font(color="FFFFFF", bold=True, size=9, name="Calibri")
PARAM_FILL  = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="D9E1F2")   # light blue
PARAM_FONT  = Font(bold=True, size=9, name="Calibri", color="1F3864")
VANT_FILL   = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="FFF2CC")   # light gold
VANT_FONT   = Font(size=9, name="Calibri", italic=True)
TITLE_FONT  = Font(bold=True, size=16, name="Calibri", color="1F3864")
SUBTITLE_FONT = Font(bold=True, size=11, name="Calibri", color="1F3864")
NOTE_FONT   = Font(size=9, name="Calibri", italic=True, color="595959")
BODY_FONT   = Font(size=9, name="Calibri")
BODY_BOLD   = Font(size=9, name="Calibri", bold=True)

WRAP_TOP    = Alignment(wrap_text=True, vertical="top", horizontal="left")
WRAP_CTR    = Alignment(wrap_text=True, vertical="center", horizontal="center")
WRAP_TOPC   = Alignment(wrap_text=True, vertical="top", horizontal="center")

THIN = Side(style="thin", color="BFBFBF")
BORDER = Border(left=THIN, right=THIN, top=THIN, bottom=THIN)

STATES = ["NJ", "NY", "CT", "MA", "CO", "OR", "WA"]
STATE_FULL = {"NJ":"New Jersey","NY":"New York","CT":"Connecticut",
              "MA":"Massachusetts","CO":"Colorado","OR":"Oregon","WA":"Washington"}

STATUS_FILL = {"R": RED_FILL, "Y": YELLOW_FILL, "G": GREEN_FILL}
STATUS_FONT = {"R": RED_FONT, "Y": YELLOW_FONT, "G": GREEN_FONT}
STATUS_WORD = {"R": "NON-COMPLIANT", "Y": "AT RISK", "G": "COMPLIANT"}

def style_cell(cell, fill=None, font=None, align=WRAP_TOP, border=BORDER):
    if fill: cell.fill = fill
    if font: cell.font = font
    if align: cell.alignment = align
    if border: cell.border = border

def state_cell(ws, row, col, text, status):
    """Write a state requirement cell with status color + leading status word."""
    c = ws.cell(row=row, column=col)
    full = f"[{STATUS_WORD[status]}] {text}" if text else f"[{STATUS_WORD[status]}]"
    c.value = full
    style_cell(c, STATUS_FILL[status], STATUS_FONT[status], WRAP_TOP)

# ============================================================================
wb = openpyxl.Workbook()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# SHEET 1 — COVER & LEGEND
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
ws = wb.active
ws.title = "Cover & Legend"
ws.sheet_view.showGridLines = False
ws.column_dimensions["A"].width = 3
ws.column_dimensions["B"].width = 34
ws.column_dimensions["C"].width = 62
ws.column_dimensions["D"].width = 30

r = 2
ws.cell(r, 2, "HARTWELL & CABOT LLP").font = Font(bold=True, size=12, color="1F3864")
r += 1
ws.cell(r, 2, "Attorneys at Law  |  Newark, NJ  &  Boston, MA").font = Font(size=9, italic=True, color="595959")
r += 2
ws.cell(r, 2, "Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Matrix").font = TITLE_FONT
r += 1
ws.cell(r, 2, "Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. (EIN 82-3461097)").font = SUBTITLE_FONT
r += 2
# Privileged banner
ws.cell(r, 2, "PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL  |  ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION  |  ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT").font = Font(bold=True, size=9, color="9C0006")
r += 2

meta = [
    ("Engagement", "Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Review (Engagement Letter dated April 14, 2025)"),
    ("Lead Partner", "Catherine Ng, Partner"),
    ("Responsible Associate", "Daniel Ikeda, Senior Associate"),
    ("Client Contact", "Rebecca Thorsen, SVP & General Counsel; Marcus Delgado, Director of HR"),
    ("Deliverable Date", "May 19, 2025"),
    ("Handbook Reviewed", "Vantage Employee Handbook, Section 6: Leave Policies (Rev. January 15, 2022)"),
    ("Jurisdictions", "Current: New Jersey, New York, Connecticut.  Expansion: Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, Washington."),
    ("Basis of Analysis", "Statutes, regulations, and agency guidance in effect as of May 19, 2025."),
]
for label, val in meta:
    ws.cell(r, 2, label).font = BODY_BOLD
    ws.cell(r, 2).alignment = WRAP_TOP
    c = ws.cell(r, 3, val); c.font = BODY_FONT; c.alignment = WRAP_TOP
    r += 1

r += 1
ws.cell(r, 2, "PURPOSE").font = SUBHDR_FONT
ws.cell(r, 2).fill = SUBHDR_FILL
ws.merge_cells(start_row=r, start_column=2, end_row=r, end_column=4)
r += 1
purpose = ("This matrix compares Vantage's current paid leave policies and practices, as set forth in the "
 "Employee Handbook (Rev. Jan. 15, 2022) and as implemented in practice, against the statutory and "
 "regulatory paid leave requirements of all seven jurisdictions in which the Company operates or plans to "
 "operate. It covers Paid Sick Leave, Paid Family Leave, Paid Medical Leave, Employer Obligations "
 "(registration, contributions, withholding, recordkeeping, posting), and PTO/Vacation Payout on Separation. "
 "Each cell is color-coded to indicate Vantage's compliance status against that jurisdiction's requirement.")
c = ws.cell(r, 2, purpose); c.font = BODY_FONT; c.alignment = WRAP_TOP
ws.merge_cells(start_row=r, start_column=2, end_row=r+3, end_column=4)
r += 5

# Legend
ws.cell(r, 2, "GAP INDICATOR LEGEND").font = SUBHDR_FONT
ws.cell(r, 2).fill = SUBHDR_FILL
ws.merge_cells(start_row=r, start_column=2, end_row=r, end_column=4)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helper to build a detail sheet
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
def build_detail_sheet(title, intro, rows, col_widths):
    """rows: list of (parameter, vantage_text, [(state_text, status) x7])"""
    ws = wb.create_sheet(title)
    ws.sheet_view.showGridLines = False
    # columns: A param, B vantage, C..I states
    widths = [3, 30, 30] + col_widths  # A spacer, param, vantage, 7 states
    for i, w in enumerate(widths, start=1):
        ws.column_dimensions[get_column_letter(i)].width = w
    r = 2
    ws.cell(r, 2, title).font = TITLE_FONT
    ws.merge_cells(start_row=r, start_column=2, end_row=r, end_column=9)
    r += 1
    c = ws.cell(r, 2, intro); c.font = NOTE_FONT; c.alignment = WRAP_TOP
    ws.merge_cells(start_row=r, start_column=2, end_row=r+1, end_column=9)
    r += 3
    # header row
    headers = ["Parameter", "Vantage Current Policy / Practice"] + [f"{s}\n{STATE_FULL[s]}" for s in STATES]
    for i, h in enumerate(headers):
        col = 2 + i
        c = ws.cell(r, col, h)
        style_cell(c, HEADER_FILL, HEADER_FONT, WRAP_CTR)
    ws.row_dimensions[r].height = 30
    header_row = r
    r += 1
    for param, vant, states in rows:
        c = ws.cell(r, 2, param); style_cell(c, PARAM_FILL, PARAM_FONT, WRAP_TOP)
        c = ws.cell(r, 3, vant); style_cell(c, VANT_FILL, VANT_FONT, WRAP_TOP)
        for i, (stext, status) in enumerate(states):
            state_cell(ws, r, 4 + i, stext, status)
        r += 1
    ws.freeze_panes = ws.cell(header_row + 1, 4)
    return ws

"""Build the Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Matrix for Vantage Logistics Group.

Prepared by Hartwell & Cabot LLP. Privileged & Confidential / Attorney Work Product.
"""
import openpyxl

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
RED_FILL    = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="FFC7CE")

GREEN_FILL  = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="C6EFCE")
GREEN_FONT  = Font(color="006100", size=9, name="Calibri")

HEADER_FONT = Font(color="FFFFFF", bold=True, size=10, name="Calibri")
SUBHDR_FILL = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="2E5496")
SUBHDR_FONT = Font(color="FFFFFF", bold=True, size=9, name="Calibri")
PARAM_FILL  = PatternFill("solid", fgColor="D9E1F2")   # light blue
PARAM_FONT  = Font(bold=True, size=9, name="Calibri", color="1F3864")

WRAP_TOP    = Alignment(wrap_text=True, vertical="top", horizontal="left")
WRAP_CTR    = Alignment(wrap_text=True, vertical="center", horizontal="center")
WRAP_TOPC   = Alignment(wrap_text=True, vertical="top", horizontal="center")

BORDER = Border(left=THIN, right=THIN, top=THIN, bottom=THIN)

STATUS_FONT = {"R": RED_FONT, "Y": YELLOW_FONT, "G": GREEN_FONT}
STATUS_WORD = {"R": "NON-COMPLIANT", "Y": "AT RISK", "G": "COMPLIANT"}

def style_cell(cell, fill=None, font=None, align=WRAP_TOP, border=BORDER):
    if fill: cell.fill = fill
    if font: cell.font = font
    if align: cell.alignment = align
    if border: cell.border = border

def state_cell(ws, row, col, text, status):
    """Write a state requirement cell with status color + leading status word."""
    c = ws.cell(row=row, column=col)
    full = f"[{STATUS_WORD[status]}] {text}" if text else f"[{STATUS_WORD[status]}]"
    c.value = full
    style_cell(c, STATUS_FILL[status], STATUS_FONT[status], WRAP_TOP)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helper to build a detail sheet
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
def build_detail_sheet(title, intro, rows, col_widths):
    """rows: list of (parameter, vantage_text, [(state_text, status) x7])"""
    ws = wb.create_sheet(title)
    ws.sheet_view.showGridLines = False
    # columns: A param, B vantage, C..I states
    widths = [3, 30, 30] + col_widths  # A spacer, param, vantage, 7 states
    for i, w in enumerate(widths, start=1):
        ws.column_dimensions[get_column_letter(i)].width = w
    r = 2
    ws.cell(r, 2, title).font = TITLE_FONT
    ws.merge_cells(start_row=r, start_column=2, end_row=r, end_column=9)
    r += 1
    c = ws.cell(r, 2, intro); c.font = NOTE_FONT; c.alignment = WRAP_TOP
    ws.merge_cells(start_row=r, start_column=2, end_row=r+1, end_column=9)
    r += 3
    # header row
    headers = ["Parameter", "Vantage Current Policy / Practice"] + [f"{s}\n{STATE_FULL[s]}" for s in STATES]
    for i, h in enumerate(headers):
        col = 2 + i
        c = ws.cell(r, col, h)
        style_cell(c, HEADER_FILL, HEADER_FONT, WRAP_CTR)
    ws.row_dimensions[r].height = 30
    header_row = r
    r += 1
    for param, vant, states in rows:
        c = ws.cell(r, 2, param); style_cell(c, PARAM_FILL, PARAM_FONT, WRAP_TOP)
        c = ws.cell(r, 3, vant); style_cell(c, VANT_FILL, VANT_FONT, WRAP_TOP)
        for i, (stext, status) in enumerate(states):
            state_cell(ws, r, 4 + i, stext, status)
        r += 1
    ws.freeze_panes = ws.cell(header_row + 1, 4)
    return ws

            cc = dash.cell(r, 3+i); cc.fill = SUBHDR_FILL; cc.border = BORDER
        cc = dash.cell(r, 10); cc.fill = SUBHDR_FILL; cc.border = BORDER
        r += 1
        continue
    c = dash.cell(r, 2, label); style_cell(c, PARAM_FILL, PARAM_FONT, WRAP_TOP)
    reds = 0
    for i, st in enumerate(statuses):
        cc = dash.cell(r, 3+i, STATUS_WORD[st])
        style_cell(cc, STATUS_FILL[st], Font(bold=True, size=8, color=STATUS_FONT[st].color), WRAP_CTR)
        if st == "R": reds += 1
    cc = dash.cell(r, 10, reds); style_cell(cc, PatternFill("solid", fgColor="F2F2F2"), BODY_BOLD, WRAP_CTR)
    r += 1
# totals row
r += 1
c = dash.cell(r, 2, "TOTAL RED INDICATORS BY STATE"); style_cell(c, SUBHDR_FILL, SUBHDR_FONT, WRAP_TOP)
# count reds per state across all non-None rows
red_counts = [0]*7
yellow_counts = [0]*7
green_counts = [0]*7
for label, statuses in dashboard:
    if statuses is None: continue
    for i, st in enumerate(statuses):
        if st == "R": red_counts[i]+=1
        elif st == "Y": yellow_counts[i]+=1
        else: green_counts[i]+=1
for i in range(7):
    cc = dash.cell(r, 3+i, red_counts[i]); style_cell(cc, RED_FILL, Font(bold=True, color="9C0006", size=10), WRAP_CTR)
cc = dash.cell(r, 10, sum(red_counts)); style_cell(cc, PatternFill("solid", fgColor="F2F2F2"), BODY_BOLD, WRAP_CTR)
r += 1
c = dash.cell(r, 2, "TOTAL YELLOW INDICATORS BY STATE"); style_cell(c, SUBHDR_FILL, SUBHDR_FONT, WRAP_TOP)
for i in range(7):
    cc = dash.cell(r, 3+i, yellow_counts[i]); style_cell(cc, YELLOW_FILL, Font(bold=True, color="9C6500", size=10), WRAP_CTR)
cc = dash.cell(r, 10, sum(yellow_counts)); style_cell(cc, PatternFill("solid", fgColor="F2F2F2"), BODY_BOLD, WRAP_CTR)
r += 1
c = dash.cell(r, 2, "TOTAL GREEN INDICATORS BY STATE"); style_cell(c, SUBHDR_FILL, SUBHDR_FONT, WRAP_TOP)
for i in range(7):
    cc = dash.cell(r, 3+i, green_counts[i]); style_cell(cc, GREEN_FILL, Font(bold=True, color="006100", size=10), WRAP_CTR)
cc = dash.cell(r, 10, sum(green_counts)); style_cell(cc, PatternFill("solid", fgColor="F2F2F2"), BODY_BOLD, WRAP_CTR)
dash.freeze_panes = dash.cell(header_row + 1, 3)
print("Sheet 2 (Compliance Dashboard) built.")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Save
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
out = "multi-state-compliance-matrix.xlsx"
wb.save(out)
print(f"\nSAVED: {out}")
print(f"Red indicators: {sum(red_counts)} | Yellow: {sum(yellow_counts)} | Green: {sum(green_counts)}")"}

{"content": "# HARTWELL & CABOT LLP
## Attorneys at Law

**One Gateway Center, Suite 2400, Newark, New Jersey 07102  |  Tel (973) 555-8100**
**225 Franklin Street, 18th Floor, Boston, Massachusetts 02110  |  Tel (617) 555-4200**

---

**PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL  |  ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION  |  ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

Prepared at the request of, and for the purpose of rendering legal advice to, Rebecca Thorsen, SVP & General Counsel, Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. Distribution is limited to authorized personnel. Do not forward to third-party vendors (including PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. and Keystone Benefit Administrators) without first consulting Hartwell & Cabot LLP; non-privileged summaries may be prepared for vendor distribution upon request.

---

# MEMORANDUM

| | |
|---|---|
| **TO:** | Rebecca Thorsen, Esq., Senior Vice President & General Counsel, Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. |
| **FROM:** | Daniel Ikeda, Senior Associate; Catherine Ng, Partner (supervising) |
| **DATE:** | May 19, 2025 |
| **RE:** | Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Review — Gap Analysis and Prioritized Remediation Plan (Seven Jurisdictions) |
| **MATTER:** | Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. — Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Review (Engagement Letter dated April 14, 2025) |

---

## 1. Executive Summary

Hartwell & Cabot LLP ("H&C") has completed the multi-state paid leave compliance review of Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. ("Vantage" or the "Company") across the seven jurisdictions in which the Company operates or plans to operate: New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut (current operations), and Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, and Washington (planned expansion). This memorandum accompanies the *Multi-State Compliance Matrix* (separate spreadsheet deliverable) and sets forth (i) all material compliance deficiencies identified, categorized by severity; (ii) the legal and operational risk associated with each; and (iii) a prioritized remediation plan with action items, responsible parties, and deadlines calibrated to the Company's facility opening schedule and the July 1, 2025 target date for a revised Employee Handbook.

The review identifies a substantial number of deficiencies. The accompanying compliance matrix records **130 red (non-compliant) indicators, 28 yellow (at-risk) indicators, and 38 green (compliant) indicators** across the seven jurisdictions and five compliance dimensions. The concentration of red indicators is heaviest in the four expansion states (Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, and Washington), driven principally by the Company's failure to register with any expansion-state paid leave program and by the structural inadequacy of the Company's self-funded paid family leave benefit.

Five deficiencies are categorized as **Critical** — meaning active legal exposure, an imminent regulatory deadline at risk, or a deficiency that has already resulted in regulatory action. The most urgent is the **Colorado FAMLI registration obligation**, which may already be overdue as a result of three remote employees hired in Colorado in April 2025 ahead of the November 1, 2025 facility opening. The **Massachusetts PFML registration** is on a critical path: factoring PinnacleHR Solutions' six-to-eight-week configuration lead time, registration must be completed no later than approximately **June 1, 2025** to ensure withholding is operational by the August 1, 2025 facility opening. The pending **New Jersey DOL complaint** (*Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.*, Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117) reflects an active, already-filed enforcement matter arising from the Company's unduly narrow definition of "family member" in its paid sick leave policy. Two further critical deficiencies are structural: the **self-funded paid family leave benefit falls below the statutory minimum in all seven jurisdictions** and has never been approved as a private plan in any state, and the Company **provides no paid medical leave for an employee's own serious health condition** while simultaneously misdirecting employees who inquire about such leave to a voluntary, employee-paid short-term disability plan rather than to the state programs into which those employees are already contributing.

Ten deficiencies are categorized as **High**, and seven as **Moderate**. The High deficiencies cluster around the Company's paid sick leave policy, which was designed around a single national template and fails to satisfy the accrual-rate, cap, waiting-period, carryover, covered-use, and family-member requirements of most or all of the seven jurisdictions. The Moderate deficiencies concern recordkeeping, workplace postings, and policy-staleness issues that do not present immediate enforcement risk but will compound as the Company scales.

H&C's overarching recommendation is that the Company **cease treating its self-funded paid family leave benefit as a substitute for state programs** and instead participate in the state-administered programs in all seven jurisdictions (mandatory in Colorado), while either enhancing the self-funded benefit to serve as a clearly-labeled supplemental top-up or discontinuing it to eliminate employee confusion and dual-coverage risk. Concurrently, the Company must **immediately initiate state program registrations** in Massachusetts and Colorado, **redraft the paid sick leave and PTO policies** to satisfy the most protective state requirements (or to provide state-specific addenda), and **replace the manual spreadsheet leave-tracking system** with a dedicated leave management platform before the Oregon and Washington facilities open.

---

## 2. Background and Scope

Vantage is a Delaware corporation with its principal office in Edison, New Jersey, operating a freight brokerage, warehousing, and last-mile delivery business. The Company currently employs approximately 2,140 employees across New Jersey (890), New York (680), and Connecticut (570), with annual payroll of approximately $112.4 million. The Company is expanding into four additional states — Massachusetts (Worcester, opening August 1, 2025; 130 projected employees), Colorado (Aurora, opening November 1, 2025; 95 projected employees), Oregon (Tigard, opening February 1, 2026; 110 projected employees), and Washington (Kent, opening May 1, 2026; 85 projected employees) — adding approximately 420 employees and $23.6 million in annual payroll, for a projected total of approximately 2,560 employees and $136.0 million in payroll.

The Company's operative leave policies are set forth in Section 6 of the Employee Handbook, last revised **January 15, 2022** — more than three years before the date of this memorandum and before the enactment or amendment of several of the statutes reviewed herein. The Company self-funds a paid family leave benefit (50% of average weekly wage, capped at $850 per week, for up to eight weeks, for bonding and family caregiving only) and provides no employer-funded paid medical leave for an employee's own serious health condition. Payroll, including state-mandated leave-program withholdings for New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut, is administered by PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. ("PinnacleHR"). Leave accruals and usage are tracked manually in Excel spreadsheets maintained by two HR coordinators.

This review was conducted pursuant to the engagement letter dated April 14, 2025. As agreed, the review excludes (a) representation in the pending NJ DOL complaint (handled under a separate engagement); (b) federal FMLA compliance; (c) local or municipal paid leave ordinances; (d) drafting of the revised Handbook; and (e) vendor contract negotiation. The analysis is based on statutes, regulations, and agency guidance in effect as of May 19, 2025, and relies on the accuracy of information and representations provided by the Company and PinnacleHR. State paid leave law is a rapidly evolving area; H&C recommends periodic re-review, particularly as each expansion phase is completed.

---

## 3. Methodology and Compliance Framework

H&C compared the Company's current leave policies (as written in the Handbook and as implemented in practice, as documented in the internal email thread of March 10–17, 2025, the PinnacleHR withholding summary dated April 10, 2025, and the sample leave-tracking spreadsheets) against the statutory and regulatory requirements of each of the seven jurisdictions across five dimensions: Paid Sick Leave; Paid Family Leave; Paid Medical Leave; Employer Obligations (registration, contributions, withholding, recordkeeping, posting); and PTO/Vacation Payout on Separation. The detailed comparison, with statutory citations and color-coded gap indicators, appears in the accompanying *Multi-State Compliance Matrix*.

Each deficiency in this memorandum is categorized by severity using the framework set forth in the engagement letter:

- **Critical** — Active legal exposure exists, an imminent regulatory deadline is at risk, or the deficiency has already resulted in, or is likely to imminently result in, regulatory action, penalties, or litigation.
- **High** — Non-compliance is likely to result in enforcement action, monetary penalties, or employee claims if not remediated before operations commence in the applicable jurisdiction or within the near term.
- **Moderate** — Technical non-compliance, best-practice gaps, or policy ambiguities that may not give rise to immediate enforcement but expose the Company to risk over time or in the event of a complaint or audit.

---

## 4. Critical Deficiencies

### C-1. Colorado FAMLI Registration — Obligation Likely Already Triggered by April 2025 Remote Hires

**Deficiency.** As of the date of this memorandum, the Company has not registered with the Colorado Family and Medical Leave Insurance ("FAMLI") program administered by the Colorado Division of Family and Medical Leave Insurance. The Company has hired three remote employees in Colorado — a regional operations manager and two logistics coordinators — who began working from their homes in the Aurora and Denver metropolitan area in April 2025, approximately seven months before the Aurora facility is scheduled to open on November 1, 2025.

**Legal basis.** Colorado FAMLI (C.R.S. § 8-13.3-501 *et seq.*) requires employer registration and premium remittance. The registration and contribution obligation attaches to employers with respect to wages paid to employees who perform work in Colorado; the obligation is not contingent on the existence of a physical facility in the state. Premiums (0.90% of wages, split 0.45% employee / 0.45% employer for employers with 10 or more employees) must be remitted on Colorado wages from the date those wages are first paid. The Colorado Healthy Families and Workplaces Act ("HFWA," C.R.S. § 8-13.3-401 *et seq.*) likewise requires covered employers to provide paid sick leave from the first day of employment.

**Risk.** If the FAMLI registration obligation is triggered by the first payment of Colorado wages — which H&C views as the better-supported interpretation — the Company has been non-compliant since April 2025 and is accruing unpaid premium obligations and potential penalties with each payroll cycle. Even under the more favorable interpretation that registration is tied to the November 1, 2025 facility opening, the PinnacleHR six-to-eight-week configuration lead time requires registration to be completed no later than approximately mid-August 2025. The three remote employees are also entitled to HFWA paid sick leave (1 hour per 30 hours worked, up to 48 hours per year, usable immediately with no waiting period) from their first day of work — a benefit the Company's current policy does not provide on the required terms. This is the single most urgent registration item in this review.

**Recommendation.** The Company should (i) immediately confirm with H&C the FAMLI registration trigger date and, if the obligation has already attached, initiate registration and begin remitting premiums (and address any back premiums) without delay; (ii) provide the three remote employees with HFWA-compliant paid sick leave terms pending the Handbook revision; and (iii) provide PinnacleHR with registration confirmation no later than mid-August 2025 to ensure withholding is operational by November 1, 2025. H&C is available to assist with the registration filing and to advise on back-premium exposure.

### C-2. Massachusetts PFML Registration — Critical Path to August 1, 2025 Opening

**Deficiency.** The Company has not registered with the Massachusetts Paid Family and Medical Leave ("PFML") program administered by the Massachusetts Department of Family and Medical Leave ("DFML"). The Worcester facility is scheduled to open on August 1, 2025.

**Legal basis.** M.G.L. c. 175M requires employer registration and contribution remittance from the first day any covered individual performs work in Massachusetts. The total contribution rate is 0.88% of eligible wages (employee share approximately 0.318%; employer share approximately 0.562% for employers with 25 or more covered individuals). MA PFML covers both family leave (up to 12 weeks) and the employee's own medical leave (up to 20 weeks), with combined caps of 26 weeks, at a blended wage-replacement rate of 80% of AWW up to 50% of the state average weekly wage ("SAWW") and 50% above that threshold, capped at approximately $1,170.64 per week (2025). Massachusetts Earned Sick Time (M.G.L. c. 149 § 148C) likewise applies from the first day of operations.

**Risk.** Factoring PinnacleHR's six-to-eight-week configuration lead time, registration must be completed no later than approximately **June 1, 2025** to ensure withholding is operational on the facility's opening day. Any delay will cascade into a delay in PinnacleHR's configuration, resulting in the Company operating in Massachusetts without proper withholding in place on August 1, 2025 — exposing the Company to unpaid contributions, penalties, and the inability to provide PFML benefits to Massachusetts employees from day one. The window between the date of this memorandum and the June 1, 2025 deadline is approximately two weeks.

**Recommendation.** The Company should initiate MA PFML registration with DFML **immediately** and target completion no later than June 1, 2025. H&C recommends treating this as the highest-priority administrative action item. The Company should also confirm whether it will participate in the state program or pursue a private-plan exemption (the latter requiring DFML approval and a benefit meeting or exceeding the statutory level — which the current self-funded plan does not, see C-4).

### C-3. New Jersey DOL Complaint — Unduly Narrow "Family Member" Definition (Active Enforcement)

**Deficiency.** The Company's Paid Sick Leave policy (Handbook §6.2.6) defines "family member" as the employee's spouse, child, and parent only. This definition is narrower than the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act ("NJ ESL," N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.*) and is the subject of a pending complaint before the New Jersey Department of Labor and Workforce Development, Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117, *Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.*, received February 3, 2025. The complaint alleges that the Company denied Ms. Muñoz the use of accrued sick leave to care for her domestic partner's child.

**Legal basis.** N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 defines "family member" broadly to include the employee's child, parent, spouse, domestic partner, civil-union partner, grandparent, grandchild, sibling, the child or parent of a domestic or civil-union partner, any other individual related to the employee by blood, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship. The Company's policy excludes each of these additional categories. Separately, the policy's enumeration of covered uses (§6.2.5) is limited to the employee's own illness, medical appointment, and work injury, and omits the care of family members, preventive care, absences related to domestic or sexual violence, public-health-emergency closures, and school-related functions — all of which are required covered uses under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3.

**Risk.** The matter is already in active enforcement before the NJ DOL, which may impose fines, administrative penalties, and orders of restitution under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-7, and has reserved the right to conduct a broader investigation of the Company's sick leave practices. The same narrow definition and use limitations render the policy non-compliant in all seven jurisdictions reviewed (the family-member and covered-use definitions in New York, Connecticut, Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, and Washington are comparably broad). The deficiency is therefore not isolated to New Jersey; it is a company-wide policy defect that exposes the Company to similar complaints in every state of operation.

**Recommendation.** The Company should expand the "family member" definition and the enumerated covered uses in the revised Handbook to satisfy the broadest applicable state standard (which will achieve compliance in all seven jurisdictions). H&C notes that the Company's February 21, 2025 response to the NJ DOL represented that policy revisions would be completed by July 1, 2025; that timeline should be honored. Resolution of the Muñoz complaint itself is handled under the separate February 15, 2025 engagement; however, the compliance findings here inform the Company's response posture (see Section 8).

### C-4. Self-Funded Paid Family Leave Benefit Below Statutory Minimums in All Seven Jurisdictions; No Private-Plan Approval

**Deficiency.** The Company's self-funded paid family leave benefit (Handbook §6.3) provides 50% of the employee's average weekly wage, capped at $850 per week, for up to eight weeks, for bonding and family caregiving only. The Company has represented that this benefit is "intended to satisfy any applicable state mandate for paid family leave" (§6.3.7) and that the Company "does not participate in state paid family leave insurance programs" (§6.3.1). The Company has **not filed a private-plan or voluntary-plan application in any jurisdiction**, and the benefit falls below the statutory level in every state on each of rate, weekly cap, and duration:

| State | Statutory Rate | Statutory Weekly Cap | Statutory Duration | Self-Funded Plan |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NJ FLI | 85% AWW | ~$1,129 | 12 wks | Below on all three |
| NY PFL | 67% AWW | $1,177.32 | 12 wks | Below on all three |
| CT PL | 95%/60% tiered | ~$941 | 12 wks (+2 preg.) | Below on all three |
| MA PFML | 80%/50% tiered | ~$1,170.64 | 12 wks family | Below on all three |
| CO FAMLI | 90%/50% tiered | ~$1,100 | 12 wks (+4 preg.) | Below; **no opt-out available** |
| PLO | 100%/50% tiered | ~$1,523.63 | 12 wks (+2 preg.) | Below on all three |
| WA PFML | 90%/50% tiered | ~$1,526 | 12 wks family | Below on all three |

**Legal basis.** Each of the seven jurisdictions administers a mandatory state paid leave program. Six (NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR, WA) permit an employer to substitute an approved private or voluntary plan, but only where the plan is formally approved by the administering agency and provides benefits that meet or exceed the state program in **all respects** — wage-replacement rate, weekly cap, duration, covered leave types, and eligibility. Colorado FAMLI does **not** permit private-employer opt-out (the opt-out is limited to local government employers); private employers must participate in the state program. Because the Company has filed no private-plan application anywhere, and because the self-funded benefit is below the statutory minimum in every state, the self-funded plan does **not** lawfully substitute for any state program. Employees therefore retain their full statutory entitlements under each state program, and the Company's representation in §6.3.7 that the self-funded benefit "is intended to satisfy any applicable state mandate" is not legally operative.

**Risk.** The Company faces dual exposure: (i) employees are paying into the state programs (via PinnacleHR withholdings in NJ, NY, and CT) and are entitled to the full statutory benefit, yet the Handbook tells them the self-funded plan satisfies the mandate — creating employee confusion, potential misdirection of claims, and restitution/penalty exposure; and (ii) in the expansion states, the Company has not registered and is not providing any compliant benefit. The self-funded plan also omits qualifying reasons that several state programs cover — including the employee's own serious health condition (CT, MA, CO, OR, WA), qualifying military exigency (NY, CT, MA, CO, OR, WA), safe leave (CT, CO, OR), and bereavement (CO). The plan's eligibility threshold (12 months' employment plus 1,000 hours worked) is more restrictive than the earnings-based eligibility of every state program.

**Recommendation.** H&C recommends that the Company **participate in the state-administered programs in all seven jurisdictions** (mandatory in Colorado in any event) and reposition the self-funded benefit, if retained, as a clearly-labeled **supplemental** top-up only — or discontinue it to eliminate employee confusion and dual-coverage risk. If the Company wishes to pursue private-plan approvals in NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR, or WA, the self-funded benefit must first be enhanced to meet or exceed each state's statutory minimums in all respects, and formal applications must be filed with the respective agencies. Given the cost and complexity of maintaining seven separate approved private plans, H&C's preliminary recommendation is full state-program participation with an optional supplemental top-up. This is a structural decision that warrants a dedicated follow-up discussion with the Company's finance and HR leadership.

### C-5. No Paid Medical Leave for Employee's Own Serious Health Condition; HR Misdirection to Voluntary STD

**Deficiency.** The Company provides no employer-funded paid medical leave for an employee's own serious health condition (Handbook §6.4.1). Employees are directed to accrued sick leave (capped at 40 hours for full-time employees) and to a voluntary, employee-paid short-term disability ("STD") plan administered by Keystone Benefit Administrators, in which fewer than 30% of Connecticut hourly staff are enrolled. Critically, HR staff at the New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut facilities have been **directing employees who inquire about paid medical leave for their own conditions to the voluntary Keystone STD plan rather than to the state programs** (NJ TDI, NY DBL, CT PL) into which those employees are already contributing via PinnacleHR withholdings. The internal email thread identifies at least seven such inquiries in the prior twelve months (four in Connecticut, two in New Jersey, one in New York), including employees facing a hip replacement and a chronic autoimmune condition.

**Legal basis.** Each of the seven jurisdictions provides state-mandated paid medical leave for an employee's own serious health condition: NJ TDI (up to 26 weeks at 85% of AWW); NY DBL (up to 26 weeks at $170/week statutory minimum); CT PL (up to 12 weeks at the 95%/60% tiered rate); MA PFML medical (up to 20 weeks); CO FAMLI medical (up to 12 weeks); PLO medical (up to 12 weeks); and WA PFML medical (up to 12 weeks). Employees in NJ, NY, and CT are paying into these programs through mandatory wage withholdings and are entitled to file claims directly with the state programs. The Company's Handbook statement that it "does NOT provide employer-funded paid medical leave" is, in isolation, permissible — but only because the state programs supply the benefit. The operational misdirection of employees to a voluntary, employee-paid plan in lieu of the state programs to which they are already contributing is a serious administrative deficiency.

**Risk.** Employees who were misdirected to Keystone instead of the state programs may have grounds for retroactive claims and restitution, and the pattern is precisely the kind of practice that prompted the Muñoz complaint — employees are willing to escalate to state agencies when they believe they have not received what they are entitled to. The misdirection also creates a record of inconsistent administration across sites, which could support disparate-treatment or discrimination claims. In the expansion states, the absence of registration means no state medical-leave coverage is configured at all, leaving a complete gap for employees' own serious health conditions.

**Recommendation.** The Company should (i) issue immediate interim guidance (extending the March 17, 2025 guidance from Ms. Thorsen) directing all HR staff to inform employees inquiring about their own medical conditions of the applicable state program and to escalate uncertain cases to the Director of HR; (ii) compile the complete list of prior misdirected inquiries (in progress per the email thread) for H&C's review to assess restitution exposure; (iii) ensure that, upon registration in each expansion state, the state medical-leave program is the primary benefit and the voluntary STD plan is positioned as supplemental only; and (iv) revise Handbook §6.4 to accurately describe the state programs as the source of paid medical leave and to clarify the supplemental role of the voluntary STD plan.
